did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used machine learning and deep learning models, specifically Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA), Support Vector Machine (SVM), Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), LeNet-5, ResNet, and an ensemble CNN-SVM model, to classify skin diseases (including melanoma) using the HAM10000 and MSC10000 datasets. A Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) was also employed for data validation.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as an 'ORIGINAL ARTICLE' in the header. It presents original experimental work involving the development and optimization of an ensemble CNN-SVM model for computer-aided diagnosis of skin diseases. The study includes a methodology section detailing the use of specific datasets (HAM10000, MSC10000), preprocessing steps (GAN for fake image removal, SMOTE for resampling), and experimental results with quantitative metrics (accuracy, F1-score) comparing multiple machine learning models (LDA, SVM, CNN, LeNet-5, ResNet).

title: Application of ensemble learning models in computer-aided diagnosis of skin diseases
brief_summary: This study aims to improve computer-aided diagnosis (CADx) for skin diseases by developing and optimizing an ensemble CNN-SVM model. The researchers evaluated this ensemble alongside LDA, SVM, CNN, LeNet-5, and ResNet models using the HAM10000 and MSC10000 datasets from Kaggle. Data preprocessing involved using a GAN to remove fake images and SMOTE to address class imbalance. The models were assessed based on accuracy and F1-scores across various skin lesion classes. The ensemble CNN-SVM model achieved the highest accuracy of 92% on the resampled HAM10000 dataset, outperforming all individual models. These results demonstrate the superior efficacy of the optimized ensemble approach for skin disease classification.

lesion_types_studied.details: Studied in HAM10000 (1113 original images, resampled to 900) and MSC10000 (part of 5000 malignant images). Classified by LDA, SVM, CNN, LeNet-5, ResNet, and ensemble CNN-SVM.
Studied in HAM10000 (514 original images, resampled to 900). Classified by LDA, SVM, CNN, LeNet-5, ResNet, and ensemble CNN-SVM.
Studied in HAM10000 (142 original images, resampled to 900). Classified by LDA, SVM, CNN, LeNet-5, ResNet, and ensemble CNN-SVM.
Studied in HAM10000 (327 original images, resampled to 900). Classified by LDA, SVM, CNN, LeNet-5, ResNet, and ensemble CNN-SVM.
Studied in HAM10000 (1099 original images, resampled to 900). Classified by LDA, SVM, CNN, LeNet-5, ResNet, and ensemble CNN-SVM.
Studied in HAM10000 (115 original images, resampled to 900). Classified by LDA, SVM, CNN, LeNet-5, ResNet, and ensemble CNN-SVM. Noted for poor performance (F1-score 0.01-0.22).
Studied in HAM10000 (6705 original images, resampled to 900). Classified by LDA, SVM, CNN, LeNet-5, ResNet, and ensemble CNN-SVM. Noted for high performance (F1-score up to 0.95).
Used in MSC10000 dataset (5000 malignant images, including melanoma) for testing ensemble CNN-SVM model performance.
Used in MSC10000 dataset (5000 benign images, comprising NV, BKL, DF, etc.) for testing ensemble CNN-SVM model performance.

all_task_types.target: Skin disease diagnostic categories (e.g., Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Vascular Lesion, Actinic Keratosis, Benign Keratosis-Like Lesions, Dermatofibroma, Melanocytic Nevi) and binary malignant/benign status.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly focuses on 'Image classification' and 'skin disease classification'. Models (LDA, SVM, CNN, LeNet-5, ResNet, Ensemble CNN-SVM) predict discrete diagnostic labels for skin lesions using the HAM10000 and MSC10000 datasets. Performance is evaluated using accuracy and F1-scores for specific lesion classes.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A large dataset of dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions sourced from Kaggle. The original dataset contains 10,015 images manually labeled by expert dermatologists. In this study, it was used in three variations: Dataset 1 (original + 20% GAN-generated fake images), Dataset 2 (original without fake images), and Dataset 3 (Dataset 2 resampled using SMOTE to balance classes).

use.training: Used for training LDA, SVM, CNN, LeNet-5, ResNet, and ensemble CNN-SVM models. Specifically, the resampled version (Dataset 3) was used for final model training and evaluation.
use.testing: Used for testing model performance (accuracy and F1-score) across different preprocessing stages (with fake images, without fake images, and resampled).
use.other: Dataset 1 included synthetic fake images generated by a GAN to test the GAN discriminator's ability to remove them. Dataset 2 was the cleaned version of Dataset 1. Dataset 3 was the balanced version of Dataset 2.

datasets_used.name: MSC10000 (Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset of 10000 Images)
datasets_used.description: A dataset sourced from Kaggle consisting of 10,000 images, evenly split between benign and malignant skin lesions. It was used to validate the generalizability of the models trained on HAM10000.
classes_used: Benign (including Melanocytic Nevi, Benign Keratosis-Like Lesions, Dermatofibroma)

use.testing: Used as an external test set (Dataset 4) to evaluate the accuracy of the trained models (LDA, SVM, CNN, ensemble CNN-SVM, LeNet-5, ResNet) on unseen data.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using descriptive statistics such as accuracy, F1-score, precision, and recall.

Dataset 1 (HAM10000 with fake images).description: Original HAM10000 dataset plus 20% GAN-generated fake images (12,010 images total). Evaluated using F1-score per class and overall accuracy.
Dataset 1 (HAM10000 with fake images).notes: The ensemble CNN-SVM model achieved the highest accuracy (0.79). The DF class showed poor performance across models (F1-scores 0.01-0.22). The NV class showed high performance (F1-scores 0.72-0.95).

Dataset 2 (HAM10000 without fake images).description: HAM10000 dataset with fake images removed using GAN discriminator (10,015 images total). Evaluated using F1-score per class and overall accuracy.
Dataset 2 (HAM10000 without fake images).notes: Performance improved significantly compared to Dataset 1. The DF class F1-score improved notably, reaching 0.70 for LDA. The ensemble CNN-SVM model demonstrated the best performance across classes.

Dataset 3 (Resampled HAM10000).description: Dataset 2 resampled using SMOTE to balance classes (900 samples per category, 6,300 images total). Evaluated using F1-score per class and overall accuracy.
Dataset 3 (Resampled HAM10000).notes: All models showed significant improvement in F1-score. The ensemble CNN-SVM model achieved the highest accuracy (0.92) and highest F1-scores for most classes (e.g., 0.99 for VAS). The paper highlights this as the optimal configuration.

Dataset 4 (MSC10000).description: External test set from Kaggle's Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset of 10000 Images (5,000 benign, 5,000 malignant). Used to validate generalizability. Evaluated using overall accuracy.
Dataset 4 (MSC10000).notes: Results follow a similar trend to HAM10000 datasets. All models showed slightly higher accuracy compared to Dataset 1 and 2, likely due to the even distribution of classes in MSC10000. The ensemble CNN-SVM model maintained the highest accuracy (0.93).

architecture_used.main_model: Ensemble CNN-SVM, CNN (vanilla), LeNet-5, ResNet, LDA, SVM
architecture_used.input_size: 64x64 pixels (inferred from CNN model output shape starting with 64x64x32 after first conv layer with stride/pooling logic, though explicit input size not stated, Table 6 shows first layer input shape implied by output (None, 64, 64, 32) and filter size (4,3) suggests input was likely 64x64x3 or similar, but strictly not reported. However, standard HAM10000 images are often resized. The paper does not explicitly state input dimensions like '224x224'. Table 6 shows 'conv2d' output is (None, 64, 64, 32). With filter size (4,3) and no padding mentioned, this is ambiguous. I will return null for input_size as it is not explicitly reported in text or tables as a dimension like '224x224'.

preprocessing_techniques: GAN-based fake image detection and removal to ensure dataset authenticity
SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique) for resampling to address class imbalance
Image resizing from 600x450 pixels to 64x64 pixels using OpenCV Python Library
Noise removal using OpenCV Python Library
Image segmentation via thresholding to isolate skin lesion regions (ROIs) from the background
Normalization and standardization of the dataset

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used to address class imbalance by generating synthetic samples for minority classes. Resampled the HAM10000 dataset to 900 images per category (total 6,300 images) from the original imbalanced distribution.

A GAN generator was used to create fake images, which were added to the HAM10000 dataset (approximately 20% of the original size) to test the GAN discriminator's ability to detect and remove them. This was used for validation of data authenticity rather than standard training augmentation.

conclusions: The optimized ensemble CNN-SVM model demonstrated superior efficacy for computer-aided diagnosis of skin diseases compared to individual models (LDA, SVM, CNN, LeNet-5, and ResNet).
The ensemble CNN-SVM model achieved a maximum accuracy of 92% on the resampled HAM10000 dataset (Dataset 3), outperforming the next best model, ResNet, which achieved 88%.

Data preprocessing steps, specifically the removal of fake images using a GAN discriminator and class balancing via SMOTE resampling, significantly improved classification performance across all models.
The ensemble CNN-SVM model maintained high performance on the external MSC10000 dataset (93% accuracy), confirming its generalizability to unseen data.

Classification performance varied significantly by lesion type; the models performed poorly on Dermatofibroma (DF) and Actinic Keratoses (AKIEC) classes, particularly in the presence of fake images, while performing well on Melanocytic Nevi (NV) and Vascular Lesions (VAS).
The study highlights the importance of data authenticity and class balance in improving the accuracy of CADx systems for skin disease detection.
important_remarks: The study relies on synthetic data manipulation (GAN-generated fake images) to validate data cleaning, which does not reflect real-world clinical data contamination risks.
Class imbalance was addressed via SMOTE resampling to exactly 900 samples per class, which may artificially inflate performance metrics and reduce ecological validity compared to natural prevalence.
Image resolution was significantly reduced from 600x450 to 64x64 pixels, potentially discarding fine-grained diagnostic features critical for dermatological analysis.

The external validation dataset (MSC10000) was limited to a binary classification task (benign vs. malignant), preventing assessment of the model's ability to distinguish between specific skin disease subtypes in unseen data.
The HAM10000 dataset used in this study was artificially augmented with 20% fake images for testing purposes, deviating from the standard clean dataset usage.
Performance on the Dermatofibroma (DF) class was notably poor in the presence of fake images (F1-score 0.01-0.22), indicating sensitivity to data quality issues for minority classes.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was reported to support the significance of performance differences between models.
The ensemble model weights (CNN 0.7, SVM 0.3) were optimized on the training set, raising potential concerns about overfitting to the specific resampled distribution.
